title: IPO
description: Suppose LeetCode will start its IPO soon. In order to sell a good price of its shares to Venture Capital, LeetCode would like to work on some projects to increase its capital before the IPO. Given n projects where the ith project has a pure profit profits[i] and a minimum capital of capital[i] is needed to start it. Initially, you have w capital. When you finish a project, you will obtain its pure profit and the profit will be added to your total capital. You can at most select k distinct projects and each project can be done at most once. Your goal is to maximize your final capital. Return the maximized capital.
thinking_questions: You can do at most k projects. Greedy: at each step, which project should you pick?
Among all projects you can currently afford (capital ≤ w), which one maximizes w?
How do you efficiently find the most profitable project among those you can afford?

hints.L1: Greedy: always pick the most profitable project you can currently afford. Use a max-heap of available profits.
hints.L2: Sort projects by capital. Use a pointer and min-heap of capitals to 'unlock' projects as w grows. Use max-heap of profits for picking.
hints.L3:
# INSIGHT: greedy + two heaps
import heapq
projects = sorted(zip(capital, profits))  # sort by capital
max_heap = []  # available profits (negated for max-heap)
i = 0
for _ in range(k):
    # unlock all projects with capital <= w
    while i < len(projects) and projects[i][0] <= w:
        heapq.heappush(max_heap, -projects[i][1])
        i += 1
    if not max_heap: break  # no affordable projects
    w -= heapq.heappop(max_heap)  # take most profitable
return w
# Time: O(n log n)  Space: O(n)

follow_up_questions: Why is greedy correct here — why doesn't taking a less profitable project first ever help?
What data structure replaces the O(k·n) naive approach and why?
How does the capital-sorted array + growing pointer work to unlock projects?
solution_notes: Sort by capital. Pointer unlocks affordable projects into max-heap. Pick most profitable each round. O(n log n).
